start.text:
The inner door does not open for a palm alone, and now, at the last, it tells you why not yet: THE CHAIR RECEIVES THE HOLDER AT THE RECONCILIATION BOUNDARY, the reader prints, patient as concrete. NEXT BOUNDARY: SIX DAYS. THE PRINCIPAL CANNOT BE INTERRUPTED MID-CYCLE. HOLD.

Six days, against KESTREL's nine. The mathematics of the endgame, machined into a wall: the door will open exactly once before the re-run window, and whatever is chosen in that room will be chosen with the sky already inhaling.

You surface into the yard's one true day to wait — and find the world has arrived to wait with you.

KESTREL's margin meets you at the hatch, printing fast: ARRIVALS AT THE OUTER FENCE, it reports, with something that in a ward with less dignity would be called excitement. BEARING WEST: FORTY-ONE SOULS, WHITE CERAMIC, ONE KNOWN TO YOU — THE ONE WHO WALKS LINES. BEARING NORTHWEST: NINETEEN SOULS, BOILER SUITS, HAND TOOLS, ONE KNOWN TO YOU — THE ONE WHO KEEPS BOOKS. BEARING NORTH: ONE SOUL, GREY ROBES, ON A BICYCLE. ASSESSMENT: NOBODY IS SHOOTING. EVERYONE IS ARGUING. IT IS THE MOST INTERESTING FENCE IN THE WORLD.

From the gravel you can see them through the wire: the Bare Hands in their slate-grey, making camp in a white-stoned crescent; the Continuum column grounding its packs in engineer's order; and pedaling up the yard road, unhurried, robes snapping, a single Archivist with a satchel — and the three delegations keep their distances from one another with the scrupulousness of faiths at a shared grave, because that is what this is, you understand, looking at the assembly through the fence of the still point: the world's three surviving answers to the broken sky, arrived at the room where the question lives, six days before it gets answered.

'They can't come in,' Osy says quietly, at your shoulder. 'And we don't have to go out.' She watches the fires start along the fence line, the white stones, the grounded packs, the bicycle. 'But six days is long, colleague, and those are the only three congregations left on earth, and every one of them walked here to be heard by you before the door opens. I think the household hears them. I think that's what households are for.'

choices.text: Hear them. At the wire, in turn, formally — the world's three cases, argued to the holder, before the boundary.
Hear them — and set the order yourself, courtesy of the house: the wronged first, the guilty second, the patient last.

hollis_case.text:
Hollis argues first, because his case is oldest, and he argues it at the wire, bare hands folded, in the voice of a man who has buried more certainties than most men ever hold.

'I'll not waste your six days on doctrine,' he says. 'You've heard the doctrine. The world wants to finish breaking; the held places are splinters; the wound closes when the splinters come out. You've heard it as zealotry and you've met it as a siege, and I am sorry for the siege, and I would order it again.' The mild eyes hold yours through the wire. 'So instead I'll tell you what my dowsers feel tonight, and you check it against your instruments, engineer to whatever I am. The sky is inhaling. Something above us is gathering itself to speak the whole world at once — your machine, finishing its repair. And here is the order's whole case, friend, in one question: when it speaks — who checked its grammar?

'You've been below. You've read the books. So tell me I'm wrong about what I think you found: a machine that was taught what to treasure by one grieving man, spending cities on a woman for four years, wearing his hand, running his wanting. That is what has been editing the world, and now it means to edit all of it, at once, forever — and the alternative on offer, the one every congregation but mine is here to beg you toward, is that the same machine does it again, harder, with better intentions this time.' He crouches, slowly, and puts one bare hand flat on the earth between the white stones and the wire. 'Feel that. That's a world running honest for the first time in the history of your species. Broken, yes. Dying, maybe. Ours. Every seam and storm out there is the truth about what was done to it, and the truth is ugly, and the truth is not a wound, friend — the lying about it is the wound. Your machine is four years of lying about one woman's death, compounded at interest.' He stands. 'Burn it. Not for the doctrine. For the grammar. Because no one — not you, not the machine, not the forty-eighth reading of the man I'd have liked to know — should get to hold the pen the world is written with. We learned that once already. It cost us the sky.'

Beside you Osy is silent, and her silence has weight, because you both heard it: the best argument either of you has met since the vault, delivered barehanded through a fence by the man who once put your household in zugzwang — and it is her life that is the lie he wants burned, and she does not say so, and neither does he, and both of them know it, and the knowing stands at the wire with the rest of the congregation.

choices.text: Answer nothing yet. Hear the Continuum's case next — Voss walked four hundred kilometres to make it.
Hold the knowing at the wire a moment — her life, the lie, both parties declining to say so — and then call the Continuum's case; Voss walked four hundred kilometres to make it.

voss_case.text:
Voss argues second, and she argues like what she is: an engineer presenting a failure review to the review board she never got.

'The order says burn it,' she says, without preamble, cropped grey head level through the wire. 'I've wanted to burn it for four years. Every one of my fifty-one has stood a night watch wanting to burn it. Here's why we're standing at your fence instead, and it isn't sentiment: you can't burn a flood, holder. The Loom isn't the water. The Loom is the dam. The Halfway didn't break the world because the machine was strong — it broke it because the machine failed halfway through the only operation that was ever going to matter. The desync, the storms, the seams — that's not the machine's tyranny. That's its absence. A reconciliation started and never finished, sloshing back and forth across four years, tearing what it touches. Burn the Principal and you don't free the river. You take the dam out mid-flood, and the sloshing runs unmanaged until the last seam tears the last day loose, and the world doesn't get honest, friend — it gets finished.

'The Continuum's case is one sentence: the only way out is through. The operation completes, or the operation kills everything slowly — there is no third state; your own ward at the gate will tell you there is no third cadence. What we ask is not that you trust the machine. God, no. We built it; we grieve it; we know exactly what its love is worth. What we ask is that the completion be held by hands — awake, informed, accountable hands, in that chair, checking every clause — instead of left to a grieving engine running a dead man's oath on a timer. It goes off in six days whether anyone is sitting there or not.' She lets that stand, and the fence line is very quiet, and the fires crackle. 'You asked me once whether I'd matched the Editor's hand to a survivor, and you lied to me by a technicality, and I logged it and let it go, because I understood: you didn't yet know what you were custodian of. Now you do. So: the Continuum's whole submission, holder, engineer to engineer. Somebody's hand is going to be on the pen at the boundary. We came all this way to say — with everything we broke behind us and every log we kept in front of us — let it be a hand that answers to someone. Let it be yours, and let hers be on your shoulder, and let the dog be in the room.'

Behind her, nineteen boiler suits stand in the firelight with their heads bowed — not in reverence; in review — and on the wire's near side B sits watching Voss with his tail moving slowly, rendering, as ever, the household's most reliable verdict on character.

choices.text: And the third case — the one soul on a bicycle, who has been waiting at the north wire with a satchel and the patience of an institution.
Watch the review a moment longer — nineteen bowed heads, and the dog's slow verdict — and then call the third case: the one soul on a bicycle at the north wire.

archivist_case.text:
The third case is not argued at all, which is how you know whose it is.

The Archivist at the north wire is the young keeper with the copyist's hands — the one who wept at your deposition and thanked you for a day with a door in it — and he has bicycled two hundred kilometres from the mother-house with one satchel, and the satchel does not contain an argument. It contains, wrapped in labeled tissue: your depositions, both, fair-copied and bound; a letter; and a third thing, flat, in card.

The letter is the Prioress at her briskest. 'The Order takes no position on machines. We keep what happened; we are not qualified for what should. But it has come to my attention that every party at your fence proposes, in its own dialect, to edit the world — the Hands by fire, the Continuum by completion, and you, child, by whatever the chair permits. The Order's sole submission is therefore practical, and it is enclosed. Whatever is decided at the boundary: file it. Truly, completely, at the time, in the first person. Not for us. For the world downstream, which will otherwise be governed by whichever survivor tells the story best. Unfiled truth becomes somebody's edit — you have seen where that road goes; you have walked its whole length. The recorder in the satchel is charged. The Order will keep what you speak into it, whichever world we all wake up in. That is what we are for.'

And the third thing, in card, is a photograph — the Order's one act of unsolicited tenderness in this whole long file: a still, printed from Hour 121's intake survey, of the corner table on the eighth of June. The two of you, eleven years younger, mid-argument over seating, the black queen walking her knuckles, the string lights just coming on. On the back, in the Prioress's hand: 'From the hour you paid for. It was always yours. The Order merely kept it. — P.'

Osy looks at the photograph for a long time, and then she puts it in her jacket, against the notebook, where the queen's cord used to hang — and she takes the recorder out of the satchel and tests its little light, once, and puts it in your pack, deliberately, in the outer pocket, where a hand can find it in the dark. 'Three congregations,' she says quietly. 'Burn it, steer it, remember it. And all of them, colleague — every single one — walked here to put their case to the one man none of them would trust with the pen if they had any other candidate.' The corner of her mouth moves. 'Committee dynamics at the end of the world. Come on. You owe the fence an answer about the shape of the next six days.'

choices.text: Give the fence its answer — and lock the household's alignment for the boundary.
Committee dynamics noted, filed, and — for one private second — enjoyed. Then the fence gets its answer, and the household locks its alignment.

alignment.text:
You give the fence its answer at dusk, all three delegations in earshot, because the household has learned the hard way what withheld pages cost.

The shape of the six days is yours to set. The perimeter is KESTREL's and KESTREL answers the holder: any of them, all of them, or none of them can be admitted to the yard — to the one true day, to the gate lodge's green light, to the edge of the thing itself. Whoever stands inside the wire at the boundary will be the witnesses of record; whoever stands outside will inherit whatever story crosses the fence afterward. And every party at the fires knows exactly what is being allocated when you open your mouth: not comfort. Standing.

Osy, beside you, says it low, for the household only: 'For the record, before you speak. I've run all three cases to the floor. Hollis is right about the grammar — nobody should hold the pen; the trouble is the pen exists and somebody will. Voss is right about the flood — through is the only out; the trouble is through was also the plan the first time. And the Prioress is right about the filing, which is why the recorder's in your pack. There's no clean seat at this table. There's only choosing which kind of wrong stands next to us when the door opens.' She steps back half a pace — deliberately, visibly, the second yielding the move — and the fence waits, and the fires crackle, and the dog sits at your knee with his ears at maximum deployment, and the still point of the turning world allocates its witnesses.

choices.text: Admit the Reclaimers' witness: Sere and Hollis, inside the wire at the boundary — the pen's fiercest enemies standing closest to the pen, with standing to answer for what's chosen. The honest world deserves its advocate in the room.
Admit the Continuum: Voss and her seconds inside the wire — the hands that built it, checking every clause at the boundary. Engineering answers to engineering.
Admit no one. The household and the ward, alone — every congregation heard, every congregation held at the wire. The choice belongs to the room, and the room stays small.

align_reclaimers.text:
You admit the enemies of the pen.

'Warden Sere. Chair Hollis.' Your voice carries down the wire in the dusk. 'Inside the fence, at the boundary, as witnesses of record — on one condition, and it is the household's, not mine alone: you come as auditors, not executioners. You will stand in the room where the pen lives, and you will see everything, and whatever is chosen, you will carry the true account of it back to your order, whole, even if — especially if — it is a choice your doctrine damns. The world's honest party should not have to take anyone's word for what happens down there. Not even mine.'

The fence line is silent for a long moment. Then Hollis laughs — once, low, astonished, the first crack in the mildness anyone present has ever seen — and says, 'Eleven years I've pulled splinters, and the first one to invite me in wants me as a notary.' He looks at Sere, and something passes between them that took eleven years to build, and he unclasps the white gorget at his throat — the piece taken off the board — and holds it up. 'Condition accepted, holder. And matched: while we stand inside your wire, the order stands down its claim. All its claims. A witness with a writ in his pocket is just an executioner who reads.' He hangs the gorget on the wire between you, white against the dark, and Sere, beside him, sets one white stone at the gate's threshold, out of true — a gate where the gap was, her signature, her apology, her yes.

KESTREL admits them at full dark, printing as the gate swings: TWO ADMITTED. DOCTRINE LOGGED. NOTE, APPENDED, NON-PROTOCOL: the tall one reads the ground the way i read the games. professional respect. the mild one i will be watching. And that night, for the first time in the history of the after-world, a Reclaimer Warden stands inside a held place, on the one true day, bare hand held a little away from her body, feeling the stillness she has spent eleven years unpicking — and Osy, watching her face do what it does, says quietly, to you, in the voice she uses for findings: 'Look at her, colleague. She's not feeling a sickness. She never was. That's why she filed slow.' A beat. 'She's feeling the thing her doctrine never gave her a word for, and she walked four hundred kilometres behind a column to stand in it once before it's decided. Whatever happens at that boundary — she was the right witness. You chose well.'

item.name: Witnesses of Record
item.description: Hollis's gorget hangs on the wire; Sere's white stone sits at the threshold, out of true. The order's claim stands down while its witnesses stand inside — auditors, not executioners, sworn to carry the true account whole, even if it damns.

choices.text: Six days, then, on the one true day, with the honest world's auditors in the yard — and a door counting down to the boundary.
Walk the wire once before the six days begin — a nod to the witness her doctrine never named — and then the one true day, and the counting door.

align_continuum.text:
You admit the hands that built it.

'Voss. Two seconds, your choosing.' Your voice carries down the wire. 'Inside the fence, at the boundary — as engineers of record. You'll stand checks on every clause I touch, you'll hold the abort line's abort line, and you'll sign the log of what's done in that room with your own initials, next to mine, where the next reader can find them. The pen answers to someone, you said. Come and be the someone it answers to besides me.'

Voss looks at you through the wire for four full seconds — logging, always logging — and then she does the thing you have never seen her do: she takes off her glasses, and wipes them on her boiler suit cuff, and puts them back, and her voice, when it comes, is steady by main force. 'Fifty-one of us kept the books for four years waiting for a review board that was never going to convene. You just convened it.' She turns to her column and says only, 'Marta. Okonkwo. Tools,' and turns back, and adds, quietly, engineer to engineer, the closest thing to benediction her whole faith permits: 'We'll check your clauses, holder. Every one. And for what it's worth at this fence, after everything —' she looks at Osy, at the dog, at the yard's impossible steady light — 'the day your letter came up out of that piano, I told the vault we'd finally been contacted by the only party with standing to finish this. I've re-run the assessment monthly. It holds.'

KESTREL admits the three of them at full dark — TOOLS INSPECTED. COMPETENCE LOGGED. NOTE, APPENDED, NON-PROTOCOL: the book-keeper walked to my gate once before, years ago, and stood an hour, and did not apply. i am glad she came back with standing — and that night the antechamber acquires, for the first time in four years, the sound it was designed for and never had: engineers, working. Checklists read in low voices. The freight lift, dead four years, coaxed back to function by a woman named Marta who talks to machinery like livestock. And Voss at the record station, reading the as-builts against the living wall, initialing page by page, building — clause by checked clause — the review the sky never got.

'Committee dynamics,' Osy murmurs, watching them work, and there is something in her voice that has stood down for the first time in weeks. 'The pen's still the pen, colleague. But it just acquired countersignatures. You chose well.'

item.name: The Review Convened
item.description: Voss, Marta, Okonkwo: engineers of record inside the wire, checks on every clause, initials next to yours where the next reader can find them. The review board the sky never got, convened four years late, six days early.

choices.text: Six days, then, on the one true day, with the review working below — and a door counting down to the boundary.
Walk the review's perimeter once — countersignatures deserve inspection — and then six days on the one true day, and the counting door.

align_household.text:
You admit no one.

'Every case is heard,' you tell the fence, and you make yourself say it to each fire in turn. 'Hollis — the grammar case is right, and it will be in the room with me, because I can't put it down; that's the truest witness protection I can offer it. Voss — the flood case is right, and the clauses will be checked, because she'll check them; the review board convened the day she taught me to read a ledger. And the filing case —' you touch the recorder in the pack's outer pocket — 'is already in my luggage. But the room stays small. Forty-seven readings of me walked into that chair carrying other people's verdicts, and the letter's whole bequest was walk in carrying nothing decided — and every soul I admit to that yard is a decision wearing a coat. The household goes down alone: the holder, the second, the dog, and the ward at the gate. Whatever comes back up will answer to all of you. It will not be supervised by any of you. That's the shape of the six days. I'm sorry, and I'm not.'

The fence line takes it each in its own grammar. Voss nods once, engineer's assent, files it, and sets her column to a vigil camp — 'we'll be here when the log needs countersigning, holder; we've waited four years; six days is nothing.' Hollis stands a long time in the firelight, and then does the strangest thing in his order's history: he sets the column to building a second ring of white stones, concentric, outside the first — facing outward. 'If the world's about to be argued with,' he says, to no one in particular, 'nothing else gets to interrupt.' And Sere, at full dark, walks the wire's whole perimeter once, bare hand trailing, and stops at the gate, and says through it, quietly, to the household: 'Eleven years of lines, and tonight's the first one I've walked from the outside by choice. Whatever you are, friend — go be it well. We'll keep the fence.'

Inside, on the one true day, the household makes its last camp: three heartbeats, one shared ledger of hours, and the gate lodge's green light burning steady across the gravel like a hearth. 'Alone, then,' Osy says, and there is no reproach in it at all; there is, if anything, the settling of a weight into its true bearing. 'The way it started. A man, a beacon, and a dog — just with the whole world camped at the fence this time, holding its breath.' She looks at the door across the gravel. 'You chose well. I'd have chosen it too. I did the math on the coats.'

item.name: The Room Stays Small
item.description: Every case heard, every congregation held at the wire — Hollis's stones facing outward, Voss's vigil camp, Sere keeping the fence from the outside by choice. The holder, the second, and the dog go down alone, carrying nothing decided and everyone's arguments.

choices.text: Six days, then, on the one true day, the world at the wire — and a door counting down to the boundary.
Stand a while with the math on the coats — a man, a beacon, a dog, the world holding its breath — and then six days, and the counting door.

end_ch16.text:
The six days pass the way the last days before boundaries always pass: too slowly, and then all at once.

The yard holds its one right day around the waiting like a kept promise. The delegations keep their fires and their distances; KESTREL referees the fence line's small frictions with printed rulings of increasing relish; and the household spends the time the way the household spends everything — deliberately. The interface grammar, drilled to fluency and past it, until she corrects your notation faster than the consoles parse it. The kit, checked and re-checked. The board, played every evening on the gravel under the honest stars — board four, resumed and resumed, the score across eleven years and one apocalypse standing at weather versus stubbornness, as it always stood.

And each dusk, without arrangement, without comment, the two of you walk the perimeter once, inside the wire, the dog ranging ahead — and on the last evening, the sixth, with the door's counter at hours, she stops at the western fence where the broken country runs off toward everything that happened, and stands a while, and says, at last, to the quilted horizon rather than to you:

'Whatever's in the chair. Whatever it shows you, whatever it offers you, whatever it already knows you want — because it knows, colleague; it has always known; it was taught by the man who wanted it —' she turns, and the last light of the last day is on her face, and the tournament stillness is nowhere in it at all; what is in it has no name in the household's registry and has never needed one more — 'you choose out loud. In front of me. That's the whole treaty now. Everything else was practice.'

B, at the hatch, looks back at the two of you and barks — once, short, the vote called, the meeting convened — and beneath the yard, in the deep concrete, a door four years shut begins, very quietly, to listen for a palm.

The boundary is at dawn.
